title: Knapsack Problem

content.problemStatement:
Let's now implement dynamic programming to solve this problem.

You have a knapsack that can take upto WmaxW_{max}Wmax​ weight. 

Given NNN items, ithi^{th}ith of them having the weight WiW_iWi​ and value ViV_iVi​. You can pick some or all the items completely so that their weight does not exceed WmaxW_{max}Wmax​. 

Your task is to maximise the total value of the items you will carry in your bag.

Note that you cannot take any item in fractions; that is, you either pick it or don't.


Input Format

The first line contains a two integer NNN and WmaxW_{max}Wmax​, denoting the length of array AAA and the maximum weight you can carry.
Next NNN lines contains the description of the items.

ithi^{th}ith line contains two integers WiW_iWi​ and ViV_iVi​, denoting the Weight and Value of the ithi^{th}ith item




Output Format
Output the maximum value you can carry while not exceeding the weight limit of the knapsack.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers N and Wmax, the number of items and the maximum weight the knapsack can hold. Then N lines follow, each containing two integers Wi and Vi, the weight and value of the i‑th item.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the maximum total value that can be carried without exceeding the weight limit.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Choosing items (5,6), (6,6) and (3,5) gives total weight 14 ≤ 15 and total value 17, which is optimal.
The best feasible item is the third one (weight 6, value 120). No other combination fits within weight 10 and yields a higher value.
Items 1, 2 and 4 (weights 1+2+4=7) give total value 6+10+15=31, which is the maximum possible.

solution.approach: The problem is the classic 0/1 knapsack. For each test case we maintain a one‑dimensional DP array dp[w] = maximum value achievable with total weight exactly w (or less). We iterate over items and update the array backwards so that each item is used at most once. After processing all items, dp[Wmax] holds the answer. This runs in O(N·Wmax) time and O(Wmax) extra space, which easily fits the given limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * Wmax) per test case, O(total N * Wmax) over all test cases.

code.Java: import java.io.*;import java.util.*;public class Main{public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException{BufferedReader br=new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));StringBuilder sb=new StringBuilder();int T=Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());while(T-- > 0){StringTokenizer st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int N=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int Wmax=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int[] dp=new int[Wmax+1];for(int i=0;i<N;i++){st=new StringTokenizer(br.readLine());int w=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());int v=Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());for(int cap=Wmax; cap>=w; cap--){int cand=dp[cap-w]+v;if(cand>dp[cap])dp[cap]=cand;}}sb.append(dp[Wmax]).append('\n');}System.out.print(sb.toString());}}

    elif i == 1:
